items.myth: Bias in ML always means unfair treatment of demographic groups.
items.reality: In the bias–variance framework, bias is estimation bias — systematic prediction error from model assumptions. Fairness bias is a separate ethics topic.
items.example: A linear model under-predicting curved housing prices has high estimation bias even if predictions are demographically fair.

items.myth: Zero training error means zero bias.
items.reality: Zero training error usually indicates low bias on the training set but can coexist with high variance; bias is about E[ŷ] vs f*, not a single fit on one dataset.
items.example: A deep tree hits 0 train error yet high val error — low bias on train, high variance; average fits across resamples may still overfit noise.

items.myth: More data always improves model performance.
items.reality: More data reduces variance; it does not remove bias from a model that cannot represent the true function.
items.example: Fitting y = x² with a line: doubling data halves variance uncertainty but the line still misses the curve — bias² unchanged in limit.

items.myth: High bias and high variance cannot occur together in practice.
items.reality: A misspecified model can underfit signal (bias) while still fitting noise differently on each sample (some variance); diagnosis uses train vs val curves, not a single label.
items.example: Small random forest with wrong features: mediocre train score (bias) and unstable val score across seeds (variance).
